subject: English Language Arts

secondary_architecture_support.note: A condensed single-period adaptation is possible, but the two-day sequence remains primary because students need planning, checkpoint, differentiated worksheet support, and final writing stages.

materials_control_note: Planning materials and checkpoint materials remain teacher-controlled until the final response release point.

lesson_overview.overview: Students explore a familiar school issue across two class days, study a short evidence set, gather reasons and evidence in aligned task-sheet variants, complete a teacher checkpoint, and then write a final argument paragraph in the declared final evidence location.
lesson_overview.essential_question: How can we use reasons and evidence to write a clear argument about a community issue?

sequence.focus: Introduce the issue, study the evidence set, gather reasons and evidence, and prepare for the teacher checkpoint.
sequence.carryover: Student planning from the task sheet continues into Day 2.

sequence.focus: Re-open planning, strengthen explanation, and write the final argument paragraph in the final response sheet.
sequence.carryover: Day 1 planning stays available while students draft their final response.

integrity_checks: Teacher-only checkpoint guidance stays in teacher-facing outputs.
Student planning work stays in task-sheet variants until the final response release point.
Final evidence stays in the final response sheet, not in teacher materials or slide notes.
Differentiated task-sheet variants remain aligned to the same learning goal and final evidence target.

teacher_guide.big_idea: Argument writing becomes clearer when students gather reasons and evidence before drafting, explain how evidence supports a reason, use a checkpoint to refine their thinking, and keep final evidence in a declared student-facing artifact.
learning_goals: I can guide students through a two-day argument-writing sequence without collapsing planning and final evidence into the same artifact.
I can use aligned worksheet variants without changing the lesson outcome.
I can use a checkpoint to strengthen student reasoning before final writing.
I can keep final written evidence in the declared final response location.
teacher_notes: Do not release the final response sheet on Day 1.
Keep sentence starters and planning supports embedded in the student-facing artifacts.
Use aligned support/core/extension task sheets to vary access without changing the final evidence target.

slides.title: Writing About a Community Issue — Day 1

content.subtitle: Launch the issue, study the evidence set, and plan your argument.

content.scenario: A school is deciding whether to make lunch periods longer. Some students say it would improve focus and reduce stress. Others say it would create scheduling problems. How could we build a clear argument about this issue?
prompts: What opinion might someone have about this issue?
What reasons might support that opinion?
What evidence would make the argument stronger?

task_sheet_supported.title: Day 1 Task Sheet — Support
student_voice_opportunity: state the issue in your own words
task_sheet_supported.choice_structure: Students choose which reason feels strongest and identify one weak spot to improve.
task_sheet_supported.differentiation_intent: Increase scaffold visibility while preserving the same planning outcome and checkpoint target.
instructions: Use the scaffold labels and frames beside the writing spaces.
Explain how the evidence supports your reason, not just what the evidence says.

tasks.label: Part A — State your opinion
tasks.prompt: What is the issue in your own words, and what do you think should happen? Frame: I think ___ should happen because ___.

tasks.label: Part B — Choose your reasons
tasks.prompt: What are two reasons that support your opinion? Label them Reason 1 and Reason 2. Star the strongest reason.

tasks.label: Part C — Gather evidence
tasks.prompt: Choose at least one useful detail for each reason. Use the labels Reason 1 evidence and Reason 2 evidence.

tasks.label: Part D — Explain your evidence
tasks.prompt: How does your evidence support your reason? Frame: This evidence matters because ___.

tasks.label: Part E — What still needs work?
tasks.prompt: Choose one weak spot and add a short note: my opinion, my strongest reason, my evidence match, or my explanation.

embedded_supports: I think ___ should happen because ___.
One important reason is ___.
This evidence matters because ___.
Star the reason you think is strongest.
success_criteria: I stated a clear opinion.
I collected reasons and evidence I can use tomorrow.
I explained how my evidence supports a reason.
I kept my planning in the declared task-sheet location.

task_sheet_core.title: Day 1 Task Sheet — Core
student_voice_opportunity: state the issue in your own words
task_sheet_core.choice_structure: Students select two reasons and identify which reason feels strongest right now.
task_sheet_core.differentiation_intent: Preserve the common planning sequence with balanced scaffold density.
instructions: Record reasons and evidence you can use again on Day 2.
Explain how the evidence supports your reason, not just what the evidence says.

tasks.label: Part A — State your opinion
tasks.prompt: What is the issue in your own words, and what do you think should happen?

tasks.label: Part B — Choose your reasons
tasks.prompt: What are two reasons that support your opinion? Which reason feels strongest right now?

tasks.label: Part C — Gather evidence
tasks.prompt: What detail from the evidence set supports each reason? Choose at least one useful detail for each reason.

tasks.label: Part D — Explain your evidence
tasks.prompt: How does your evidence support your reason? Do more than repeat the detail. Explain why it matters.

tasks.label: Part E — What still needs work?
tasks.prompt: What is one weak spot or question you still need to improve before writing your paragraph tomorrow?

embedded_supports: I think ___ because ___.
One important reason is ___.
This evidence matters because ___.
Box the reason you think is strongest.
success_criteria: I stated a clear opinion.
I collected reasons and evidence I can use tomorrow.
I explained how my evidence supports a reason.
I kept my planning in the declared task-sheet location.

task_sheet_extension.title: Day 1 Task Sheet — Extension
student_voice_opportunity: state the issue in your own words
judge which reason is strongest and why
task_sheet_extension.choice_structure: Students choose the strongest reason and decide which detail needs the most explanation to become convincing evidence.
task_sheet_extension.differentiation_intent: Use lighter scaffolds and more open writing space while preserving the common outcome.
instructions: Record reasons and evidence you can use again on Day 2.
Show the logic between detail and claim, not just repetition.

tasks.label: Part A — State your opinion
tasks.prompt: What is the issue in your own words, and what do you think should happen?

tasks.label: Part B — Choose your reasons
tasks.prompt: What are two reasons that support your opinion? Which reason is strongest, and why?

tasks.label: Part C — Gather evidence
tasks.prompt: What detail from the evidence set supports each reason? Which detail would need the most explanation to become convincing evidence?

tasks.label: Part D — Explain your evidence
tasks.prompt: How does your evidence support your reason? Avoid repeating the evidence; show the logic between detail and claim.

tasks.label: Part E — What still needs work?
tasks.prompt: What is one weak spot or question you still need to improve before writing your paragraph tomorrow?

embedded_supports: Choose the reason you trust most.
Avoid repeating the evidence; show the logic behind it.
Be ready to explain why one detail matters.
success_criteria: I stated a clear opinion.
I collected reasons and evidence I can use tomorrow.
I explained how my evidence supports a reason.
I kept my planning in the declared task-sheet location.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Day 1 Checkpoint Sheet
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Teacher checkpoint before Day 2 final response release.
look_fors: Student has at least two usable reasons.
Student can point to evidence or explanation for those reasons.
Student can explain how the evidence supports the reason.
conference_prompts: Show me the strongest reason in your planning.
What evidence makes that reason believable?
How does that evidence support your reason?
What still needs strengthening before you draft tomorrow?
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Release the Day 2 final response sheet only when the student can point to usable reasons and evidence in the task sheet and explain at least one connection between evidence and reason.

days.carryover_note: Use Day 1 planning before drafting the final argument paragraph.

slides.title: Writing About a Community Issue — Day 2

content.subtitle: Revisit your planning, strengthen your explanation, and write your final paragraph.

content.scenario: Before you draft, look back at the reasons and evidence you carried from Day 1.
prompts: Which piece of evidence needs clearer explanation?
How will you organize your final paragraph?

task_sheet_supported.title: Day 2 Task Sheet — Support
student_voice_opportunity: keep the reason you trust most
choose whether to improve evidence or explanation
task_sheet_supported.choice_structure: Students choose one revision path before strengthening planning for the final paragraph.
task_sheet_supported.differentiation_intent: Stronger scaffold visibility with an explicit revision path and a prominent explanation frame.
instructions: Re-read your Day 1 planning before making changes.
Choose one revision path: improve your evidence or improve your explanation.
Do not write your final paragraph on this sheet.

tasks.label: Part A — Keep your strongest reason
tasks.prompt: Which reason still feels strongest? Frame: My strongest reason is ___ because ___.

tasks.label: Part B — Revise your evidence or explanation
tasks.prompt: Choose one path: I will improve my evidence. I will improve my explanation.

tasks.label: Part C — Write one stronger explanation sentence
tasks.prompt: Write one improved explanation sentence using the frame: This evidence matters because ___.

embedded_supports: Choose the reason you trust most.
Make your evidence match your reason clearly.
Explain why the detail matters, not just what it says.
Do not write your final paragraph on this sheet.
success_criteria: I improved the planning I started on Day 1.
I know which reasons and evidence belong in my final paragraph.
I improved at least one explanation before drafting.
I kept planning separate from final evidence.

task_sheet_core.title: Day 2 Task Sheet — Core
student_voice_opportunity: keep the reason you trust most
choose what needs the strongest revision
task_sheet_core.choice_structure: Students keep the strongest reason and decide which evidence or explanation needs revision.
task_sheet_core.differentiation_intent: Balanced support while preserving the same revision sequence and final evidence target.
instructions: Re-read your Day 1 planning before making changes.
Strengthen one reason or one piece of evidence.
Improve your explanation before drafting the final paragraph.

tasks.label: Part A — Keep your strongest reason
tasks.prompt: Which reason still feels strongest, and why are you keeping it in your final paragraph?

tasks.label: Part B — Revise your evidence or explanation
tasks.prompt: What is one piece of evidence or one explanation you can improve so your opinion is supported more clearly?

tasks.label: Part C — Write one stronger explanation sentence
tasks.prompt: Write one improved explanation sentence using the frame: This evidence matters because ___.

embedded_supports: Choose the reason you trust most.
Make your evidence match your reason clearly.
Explain why the detail matters, not just what it says.
Keep planning work separate from your final paragraph.
success_criteria: I improved the planning I started on Day 1.
I know which reasons and evidence belong in my final paragraph.
I improved at least one explanation before drafting.
I kept planning separate from final evidence.

task_sheet_extension.title: Day 2 Task Sheet — Extension
student_voice_opportunity: judge which revision will make the argument most convincing
name the reason that gives the argument the most strength
task_sheet_extension.choice_structure: Students choose the revision that makes the final paragraph more convincing, not just longer.
task_sheet_extension.differentiation_intent: Lighter scaffolds and more open revision space while preserving the same final evidence target.
instructions: Re-read your Day 1 planning before making changes.
Choose the revision that will make your final paragraph more convincing.
Revise for stronger argument language, not repetition.

tasks.label: Part A — Keep your strongest reason
tasks.prompt: Which reason gives your argument the most strength, and why are you keeping it in your final paragraph?

tasks.label: Part B — Revise your evidence or explanation
tasks.prompt: Choose the change that will make your final paragraph more convincing, not just longer.

tasks.label: Part C — Write one stronger explanation sentence
tasks.prompt: Write one improved explanation sentence. Avoid repeating the evidence; show the logic behind it.

embedded_supports: Choose the reason you trust most.
Avoid repeating the evidence; show the logic behind it.
Revise for stronger argument language, not repetition.
Keep planning separate from final evidence.
success_criteria: I improved the planning I started on Day 1.
I know which reasons and evidence belong in my final paragraph.
I improved at least one explanation before drafting.
I kept planning separate from final evidence.

final_response_sheet.title: Day 2 Final Response Sheet
final_response_sheet.prompt: Write one argument paragraph that states your opinion clearly, gives reasons, and uses evidence or explanation to support your thinking.
planning_reminders: Use one or two strong reasons from your task sheet.
Explain why your evidence supports your argument.
Keep your final written answer on this sheet.
success_criteria: My paragraph states a clear opinion.
My paragraph uses reasons and supporting evidence.
My paragraph explains why the evidence supports my argument.
My final evidence is recorded in the declared final response location.
